current_fact.fact_text: On March 9, 1842, Franciscan miners reported the first documented discovery of gold in California at Rancho San Francisco, near present-day Los Angeles. The find quietly foreshadowed the massive California Gold Rush that would erupt six years later and transform the region’s landscapes and societies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

current_fact.match_reason: An early glint of gold in sunny California hints at the long summers and dreams that would draw people west, aligning with this episode’s warm, sun-drenched, coastal jazz atmosphere.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1842, Giuseppe Verdi’s opera "Nabucco" premiered at La Scala in Milan, and its rousing chorus "Va, pensiero" soon became an unofficial anthem of Italian longing and unity. The success of this third opera pulled Verdi from near-obscurity into the center of Italian musical life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: Verdi’s "Nabucco" is a milestone of Italian musical identity, and this episode is a love letter to Italian songwriters and landscapes, tying national sound, memory, and emotion together.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1960, Dr. Belding Hibbard Scribner implanted the first Scribner shunt into a patient, creating a reliable access point for hemodialysis. This simple loop of Teflon tubing transformed kidney failure from a rapid death sentence into a chronic condition that could be managed for years.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: The Scribner shunt is a story of medical innovation that gave patients time to heal and live; this reflective episode on bodily vulnerability and recovery mirrors that new lease on life.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1959, the Barbie doll debuted at the American International Toy Fair in New York, introducing a fashion doll with an adult body to a market used to baby dolls. Barbie quickly became both a global icon and a lightning rod in debates about gender roles, beauty standards, and imagination in play.

episode.title: Sedna FM #03 - Beautiful as You Feel
episode.description: An episode about self-acceptance and inner beauty. Music that reminds us we are beautiful as we feel, embracing our authentic selves. Warm, uplifting sounds that celebrate individuality.
songs: The The - this is the day
DCTL - Deep Tannin f(eat Rafael Leafar)

queue.match_reason: Barbie sits at the intersection of beauty ideals and identity; an episode about inner beauty and self-acceptance offers a gentle counterpoint to the doll’s polished surface.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 2015, two Eurocopter AS350 Écureuil helicopters collided in mid-air near Villa Castelli, Argentina, during the filming of the French reality TV show "Dropped." The crash killed all ten people on board, including three prominent French athletes, and highlighted the risks hidden behind adventure television.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A tragic accident tied to adventure and celebrity calls for a gentle, hopeful space; this episode’s theme of weathering storms and moving through difficult transitions suits the reflective tone such a memory needs.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1978, Indonesian President Suharto inaugurated the Jagorawi Toll Road, the country’s first toll highway, linking Jakarta with Bogor and Ciawi. The road became a symbol of Indonesia’s rapid modernization, reshaping how people and goods moved across Java.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: A new toll road literally puts a nation ‘on the move’; this travel-ready, kinetic mix is built for highways and motion, echoing the feeling of a fresh route opening.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1942, as part of World War II, the Dutch East Indies forces surrendered unconditionally to the Japanese Army, ending the Dutch colonial government’s control over what is now Indonesia. The brief but intense campaign reshaped power in Southeast Asia and set the stage for Indonesia’s later struggle for independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: The fall of a colony and the path toward independence resonate with this episode’s focus on music born from exile, occupation, and resistance, even though the geography differs.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1945, U.S. B-29 bombers carried out a devastating firebombing raid on Tokyo, using incendiaries that turned wooden neighborhoods into a firestorm. In a single night, over 100,000 civilians were killed and much of the city was destroyed, making it one of the deadliest air raids in history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: The horror of a city consumed by bombing ties to themes of war, power, and anger; Rage Against the Machine’s politically charged music offers a sonic language of protest and confrontation.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1916, during the Mexican Revolution, Pancho Villa led nearly 500 fighters across the U.S. border to attack Columbus, New Mexico. The raid killed soldiers and civilians, prompting a punitive expedition into Mexico and marking one of the last armed incursions on the continental U.S.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: Pancho Villa’s cross-border raid is a story of conflict and combat; pairing it with an episode about martial arts and disciplined fighting reframes violence through controlled movement and practice.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1815, Francis Ronalds’ battery clock story shared pages with other early-industrial curiosities, but elsewhere that same day Europe still trembled from the aftershocks of the Napoleonic era. Around this time, mechanical timekeeping, railways, and imperial ambitions were tightening the schedules and borders of everyday life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

queue.match_reason: The tightening grip of industrial time and empire anticipates later steel-and-smoke cities; this Sheffield-focused episode captures the industrial, clockwork pulse that would follow Ronalds’ innovations.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1796, Napoleon Bonaparte married Joséphine de Beauharnais in a civil ceremony in Paris, just days before leaving to command the French army in Italy. Their passionate, complicated relationship unfolded alongside his meteoric rise and eventual fall, intertwining love, power, and politics.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

queue.match_reason: Napoleon and Joséphine’s marriage is a tale of grand, vulnerable, and irrational love that shaped history; it fits naturally with this deep dive into love’s messy, art-inspiring side.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1500, Portuguese navigator Pedro Álvares Cabral sailed from Lisbon with a fleet bound for the Indies, following a route that would unexpectedly bring him to the coast of Brazil. His voyage, launched under the Treaty of Tordesillas, ended up expanding the map of the known world for Europeans.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: Cabral’s fleet leaving Lisbon for a long ocean voyage aligns perfectly with this episode recorded on a sailing boat, both celebrating life at sea, shifting horizons, and shared journeys.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1912 BCE-equivalent in Chinese chronology, Liu Che—later known as Emperor Wu of Han—would eventually ascend the throne (on this date in 141 BCE) and reign for 54 years. His rule expanded the Han empire through military campaigns and fostered trade routes that developed into the Silk Road, deeply influencing East Asian history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

queue.match_reason: Emperor Wu’s long reign was a time of transition, expansion, and redefining what grounded an empire; this reflective episode on shifts in rhythm and what anchors us offers a modern, intimate parallel.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1954, CBS aired "A Report on Senator Joseph McCarthy" on the program See It Now, where journalist Edward R. Murrow calmly but sharply questioned McCarthy’s tactics. The broadcast is often credited with turning public opinion against McCarthyism’s climate of fear and suspicion.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: Murrow’s report was a media ‘bomb’ dropped into the era’s fear-driven narrative; Sedna’s first raw, outspoken episode, filled with socially aware artists like Fela Kuti and Nas, echoes that spirit of speaking truth through broadcast.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 2011’s Discovery mission and the shuttle era more broadly, TV and cameras turned astronauts into global icons, but decades earlier, broadcasters like CBS were already shaping how people experienced distant events in their living rooms. The medium that carried Murrow’s McCarthy report would later carry moonwalks, shuttle launches, and the sight of Earth itself from orbit.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: The evolution of television as a window on history pairs nicely with the introduction of a new radio format; both are about opening fresh channels for experiencing distant stories and moods.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, 1910s-era Mexico and beyond, revolutions and wars pushed people across borders, but centuries earlier, the rise of empires like Han China or early European powers had already set trade and migration patterns in motion. Each wave of conflict and connection left traces in language, food, and music—the cultural "broadcasts" of their time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: Revolution, displacement, and cultural mixing shaped Mexico just as they shaped Andalusia; flamenco’s history of layered influences and resilience mirrors the cultural aftershocks of upheaval.

queue.fact_text: On March 9, across different centuries and continents—from Emperor Wu’s China to Verdi’s Milan—artists, rulers, scientists, and explorers all, in their way, tried to bend time: with clocks, with treaties, with opera, with rockets. The date becomes a tiny time capsule showing how human beings keep returning to the same questions of power, beauty, risk, and wonder.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: This meta-look at March 9 as a pattern in time pairs with an episode about movement and shifting perspective, inviting listeners to see the whole day’s stories as one long, drifting meditation through history.

published.fact_text: On March 9, 2011, Space Shuttle Discovery returned from its final mission and glided to a stop at Kennedy Space Center, closing a career of 39 flights and nearly 149 million miles traveled. Among its journeys were the Hubble Space Telescope deployment and multiple construction trips to the International Space Station, making Discovery the most flown orbiter in NASA's shuttle fleet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: Discovery’s final landing closes one era of space exploration and opens space for new ones, echoing the episode’s theme of seeing emptiness as creative space and its cosmic-tinged title, “Space Shift.”

published.fact_text: On March 9, 1974, the Soviet Mars 7 spacecraft released its lander too early, causing it to miss Mars by about 1,300 kilometers and drift into a solar orbit. Though the mission failed to land, the flyby bus still transmitted data about interplanetary space, turning a miscalculation into an unexpected kind of exploration.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: Mars 7’s missed landing feels like a cinematic near-miss in deep space, fitting the moody, atmospheric, and slightly off-kilter explorations of trip-hop in this episode.

published.fact_text: On March 9, 1961, the Soviet Union launched Sputnik 9 (Korabl-Sputnik 4), sending a dog, mice, and a human-shaped mannequin nicknamed "Ivan Ivanovich" into orbit before safely returning the capsule to Earth. This unusual test flight proved that living beings could survive launch, spaceflight, and re-entry, paving the way for Yuri Gagarin’s historic mission a month later.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: A test flight with a mannequin named Ivan orbiting Earth is pure retro-futurism, perfectly paired with “Cosmic Monday”’s machine-led, spacey disco energy.

published.fact_text: On March 9, 1997, skywatchers in China, Mongolia, and eastern Siberia experienced a rare spectacle when a solar eclipse made Comet Hale–Bopp visible in the daytime sky. The comet itself was one of the brightest of the 20th century, remaining visible to the naked eye for a record-breaking 18 months.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A daytime comet glimpsed under a darkened sky feels like a natural-world miracle, much like the quiet awe of meeting whales and orcas in polar light in this cinematic, nature-focused episode.

published.fact_text: On March 9, 1957, an enormous 8.6-magnitude earthquake struck the Andreanof Islands in the Aleutian chain, generating a tsunami that crossed the Pacific and caused millions of dollars in damage. The event helped scientists better understand subduction-zone quakes and the long reach of tsunami waves across oceans.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A massive quake and tsunami delivering sudden, destructive energy across the Pacific pairs with the episode’s theme of being jolted awake by powerful, electric guitar-driven shocks.

published.fact_text: On March 9, 1815, English scientist Francis Ronalds described the first battery-powered clock in the Philosophical Magazine. By combining electrochemistry with timekeeping, he anticipated later electric clocks and showed how electricity could quietly reshape everyday rhythms.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: Ronalds’ electric clock literally rewired how we measure time, and this episode meditates on slowing the planet’s tempo, making an elegant link between timekeeping and consciously choosing our rhythm.

published.fact_text: On March 9, 1776, Scottish philosopher Adam Smith published "The Wealth of Nations," a sprawling investigation into labor, markets, and what drives prosperity. His ideas about the division of labor, free trade, and the "invisible hand" helped define modern economics and still shape policy debates centuries later.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: Smith spent years learning, observing, and synthesizing to write a foundational text; this reflective episode about learning and long-term effort mirrors the slow, analytical work behind "The Wealth of Nations."

published.fact_text: On March 9, 1842, Franciscan miners reported the first documented discovery of gold in California at Rancho San Francisco, near present-day Los Angeles. The find quietly foreshadowed the massive California Gold Rush that would erupt six years later and transform the region’s landscapes and societies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: An early glint of gold in sunny California hints at the long summers and dreams that would draw people west, aligning with this episode’s warm, sun-drenched, coastal jazz atmosphere.
